findings.title: Live DB password, JWT secret, and SMTP password committed to git in .env.production and .env.staging
findings.description: Both .env.production and .env.staging are tracked by git (visible via git ls-files). They contain plaintext Azure SQL password, JWT_SECRET, and Resend SMTP password. The .gitignore rule '.env.*' was added AFTER the files were committed, so the ignore rule has no effect on already-tracked files.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Anyone with read access to the repo (including any former collaborator, or if repo is ever pushed public, or mirrored via a compromised CI token, or exposed via GitHub org migration) fetches .env.production. 2) They get DATABASE_URL with password Sh13ldlyt1c$ for the dev DB + prod JWT_SECRET + SMTP password. 3) They can (a) connect directly to Azure SQL and read all tenant data, (b) forge JWTs as any user in any company for the prod app, (c) send emails as support@shieldlytics.com via Resend.
findings.impact: Total compromise: cross-tenant data read/write, account takeover via forged JWT, phishing via Resend.
findings.recommendation: 1) Rotate ALL three secrets immediately: Azure SQL password, JWT_SECRET (will invalidate all sessions — expected), Resend API key. 2) Remove files from tracking: git rm --cached .env.production .env.staging then commit. 3) Scrub git history: git filter-repo --invert-paths --path .env.production --path .env.staging OR BFG Repo-Cleaner. Force-push to all remotes (staging + origin). 4) Store secrets in Azure App Service configuration (or Key Vault) and remove the pipeline step that copies .env.production into the deployment zip. 5) Audit Azure SQL audit logs and Resend dashboard for unauthorized use between first-commit date and now.
findings.playbook: Revoke → Rotate → Scrub history (git filter-repo) → Force-push → Audit exposure window → Check abuse

findings.title: Azure SQL password hardcoded in package.json npm scripts
findings.description: The 'backend' and 'server:dev' npm scripts embed DATABASE_URL with live password Sh13ldlyt1c$ inline. package.json is tracked in git and visible to everyone with repo access. Same password as .env files, confirming reuse.
findings.exploit_scenario: Same as Finding 1 — attacker reads package.json, extracts the DATABASE_URL, connects to Azure SQL directly, exfiltrates or modifies all tenant data.
findings.impact: Direct DB access independent of any server-side auth.
findings.recommendation: Remove DATABASE_URL from package.json scripts. Require developers to source .env locally (and never commit .env). Replace with: 'backend': 'bun server.cjs' and let Node load .env via dotenv or Bun's built-in env-file support.
findings.playbook: Rotate DB password → Edit package.json → Commit → Communicate rotation to team

findings.category: OWASP A01: Broken Access Control / Path Traversal
findings.title: Path traversal in /api/debug/serve-asset/:filename reads arbitrary server files
findings.description: Unauthenticated endpoint uses path.join(__dirname, 'assets', filename) where filename comes from req.params. path.join does NOT sanitize '..' sequences; it resolves them. Express auto-decodes %2F to '/'. A request like GET /api/debug/serve-asset/..%2F..%2F.env returns the .env file.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Attacker sends GET https://guardian-staging.azurewebsites.net/api/debug/serve-asset/..%2F.env. 2) Server reads the deployment .env (which the pipeline copies from .env.production) and returns it. 3) Attacker now has DATABASE_URL, JWT_SECRET, SMTP_PASSWORD. Same outcome if the attacker fetches ..%2Fserver.js (source code with auth logic), ..%2Fweb.config, ..%2Fprisma%2Fschema.prisma, etc.
findings.impact: Unauthenticated arbitrary file read from the web root. Immediate secret exfiltration. Source code disclosure enables further vulnerability discovery.
findings.recommendation: Delete the /api/debug/serve-asset endpoint entirely — it's a debug aid and should never ship. If you truly need it, (a) gate behind requireJafar, (b) whitelist the filename: `if (!/^[a-zA-Z0-9._-]+$/.test(filename)) return 400`, (c) verify the resolved path starts with the assets directory: `const resolved = path.resolve(assetPath); if (!resolved.startsWith(path.resolve(__dirname, 'assets') + path.sep)) return 400`.
findings.playbook: Delete endpoint → Verify logs for prior /api/debug/serve-asset access patterns → Rotate any secret that might have been exposed

findings.title: Unauthenticated POST /invites/send grants Super Admin to attacker-chosen email
findings.description: POST /invites/send has no auth middleware. Accepts { invites: [{email, roleId}] } from the request body — attacker fully controls the roleId (no allowlist check). companyId is hardcoded to 1. Token generated with Math.random()+Date.now().toString(36) — non-cryptographic. Resend sends the invite email; /api/invite/accept (line 9913) accepts the token and creates an active user with the invited role.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) curl -X POST https://guardian-staging.azurewebsites.net/invites/send -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"invites":[{"email":"attacker@evil.com","roleId":6}]}'. 2) Attacker receives Super Admin invite email from support@shieldlytics.com. 3) Attacker calls POST /api/invite/accept with the token + chosen password. 4) Account created with ROLE_ID=6 (Super Admin) in COMPANY_ID=1. 5) Attacker logs in as Super Admin, controls admin-only endpoints (/api/admin/permissions, form-allowlist, etc.). Requests to /api/users/* are limited to company 1 but that may already be the primary tenant.
findings.impact: Full privilege escalation to Super Admin, unauthenticated, against the production/staging app.
findings.recommendation: Delete /invites/send entirely — it's legacy. The authenticated counterpart POST /api/invites (line 3312) with getAuthenticatedUserCompany already exists for real invite sends. If you can't delete: (a) add getAuthenticatedUserCompany + requireJafar, (b) derive companyId from req.companyId, (c) validate roleId against an allowlist and against the inviter's permission to grant that role, (d) replace Math.random() with crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex').
findings.playbook: Delete endpoint → Check GUARDIAN.USERS for recently created role-6 users → Revoke suspicious accounts → Audit INVITES table
findings.verification: independently verified — endpoint exists, no auth middleware, role accepted from body, hardcoded companyId=1

findings.title: SQL injection in GET /api/requests/:requestId/milestones via sortBy, sortOrder, type query params
findings.description: Lines 5100-5193: sortBy/sortOrder/type come directly from req.query. They are interpolated into a SQL string: `ORDER BY wp.${sortBy} ${sortOrder}` and `WHERE wp.PROGRESS_TYPE = '${type}'`. The query is then executed via prisma.$queryRawUnsafe — no parameterization, no escaping on these values. Authenticated but any user in the tenant can exploit (getAuthenticatedUserCompany is the only guard).
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Any authenticated user (lowest privilege) calls GET /api/requests/1/milestones?sortBy=CREATE_DATE; SELECT * FROM GUARDIAN.USERS INTO OUTFILE .... 2) SQL Server executes: ORDER BY wp.CREATE_DATE; SELECT ... Depending on permissions of the GUARDIAN SQL user, attacker can read/write ANY table, extract password hashes, or invoke xp_cmdshell if enabled. Simpler exfil: sortBy=CREATE_DATE OFFSET 0 ROWS; SELECT USER_ID, EMAIL, PASSWORD_HASH FROM GUARDIAN.USERS FOR JSON AUTO--
findings.impact: Full database compromise via any authenticated account. Cross-tenant data leak (company isolation bypassed at DB level).
findings.recommendation: Replace all $queryRawUnsafe in this handler with parameterized $queryRaw (tagged template literal). For sortBy/sortOrder: allowlist them before interpolation — `const ALLOWED_SORTS = {CREATE_DATE: 'wp.CREATE_DATE', UPDATE_DATE: 'wp.UPDATE_DATE'}; const sortCol = ALLOWED_SORTS[sortBy] || 'wp.CREATE_DATE'; const order = sortOrder === 'ASC' ? 'ASC' : 'DESC';`. For type: pass as a parameter, not string interpolation.
findings.playbook: Audit SQL Server audit logs for suspicious queries from Guardian app IPs → Rotate DB password (again — if not already) → Patch code → Consider adding a WAF rule temporarily
findings.verification: independently verified — code path reviewed end-to-end, user input flows directly into string SQL

findings.title: Unauthenticated /api/cleanup/* endpoints allow data destruction
findings.description: POST /api/cleanup/incomplete-registrations (line 1249) and POST /api/cleanup/periodic (line 1285) have no auth. Accept timeoutMinutes / daysOld from the body. Internally call cleanupIncompleteRegistrations / performPeriodicCleanup which delete user registrations older than the threshold.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Attacker calls POST /api/cleanup/periodic with {daysOld: 0}. 2) Every 'incomplete' registration in the system is deleted — the definition of 'incomplete' needs code review but the endpoint is clearly intended for cleanup and unauthenticated callers should never trigger it. 3) If definition of 'incomplete' includes users who haven't verified email within N hours, valid mid-onboarding users are wiped. 4) Or, POST /api/cleanup/incomplete-registrations {email: 'victim@co.com', timeoutMinutes: 0} nukes specific victim's registration record.
findings.impact: Data destruction. Denial of registration. Targeted user record removal.
findings.recommendation: Gate all /api/cleanup/* endpoints behind requireJafar (or remove entirely and run cleanup as a scheduled Azure WebJob with no HTTP surface). At minimum add getAuthenticatedUserCompany + role check.
findings.playbook: Add auth middleware → Deploy → Audit GUARDIAN.USERS for unexpected deletions in audit logs
findings.verification: independently verified — routes present, no middleware before handler

findings.title: POST /api/send-error-email is an open email relay (phishing vector)
findings.description: Unauthenticated endpoint accepts {to, subject, htmlBody, errorDetails} and forwards to Resend with from=support@shieldlytics.com. The only check is rate limiting keyed by `to` — attacker bypasses by rotating recipients. Only runs in production (isProduction gate).
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker POSTs to /api/send-error-email with to=victim@target.com, subject='Your account is locked', htmlBody='Reset password'. Email arrives from support@shieldlytics.com (authentic DKIM) — high deliverability, high trust. Phishing campaign at scale by rotating recipients.
findings.impact: Phishing/spam from legitimate Shieldlytics domain, brand damage, Resend account abuse/suspension, potential customer compromise.
findings.recommendation: This endpoint should not exist in its current form. If client-side error reporting is a requirement: (a) gate behind auth + rate limit by user, (b) server constructs the email — only accept errorDetails; ignore client to/subject/htmlBody, (c) forward errors to a single internal inbox like errors@shieldlytics.com, not arbitrary recipients, (d) consider using Sentry or similar instead of email.
findings.playbook: Patch immediately (hardcode to=errors@shieldlytics.com) → Check Resend logs for suspicious send patterns → Consider Resend API key rotation if abuse observed
findings.verification: independently verified — caller controls to/subject/htmlBody; only per-recipient rate limit which attacker trivially bypasses

findings.title: Inconsistent hardcoded JWT_SECRET fallbacks ('guardian-jwt-secret-key' vs 'your-secret-key')
findings.description: Line 702 sets JWT_SECRET = process.env.JWT_SECRET || 'guardian-jwt-secret-key' (used by jwt.sign at line 1699). Line 1117 calls jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET || 'your-secret-key') — DIFFERENT fallback. If env var is missing: sign uses one default, verify uses another, all auth fails (self-healing).  But a partial env misconfiguration (one env set, one not) could create signing/verification asymmetry. More importantly: if JWT_SECRET is ever unset in production (container misconfiguration, env var wipe), the signing path uses a publicly known default from source code — attacker forges tokens.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Azure App Service slot swap misconfigures env vars → JWT_SECRET unset on one side. 2) Or, an attacker who reads server.cjs (via the path traversal finding #3) learns the default, and server is mid-deploy without env vars set. 3) Attacker crafts a JWT with `{id: 1, roles: [6]}`, signs with HS256 + 'guardian-jwt-secret-key' — server accepts.
findings.impact: Conditional auth bypass under env-var misconfiguration.
findings.recommendation: Remove both fallbacks. Refuse to start if JWT_SECRET is unset: `if (!process.env.JWT_SECRET) { console.error('JWT_SECRET required'); process.exit(1); }`. Use a single `const JWT_SECRET` exported via the module and import it at the verify site.
findings.playbook: Patch → Deploy with env var check at startup → Verify prod has JWT_SECRET set
findings.verification: independently verified via reading server.cjs lines 702 and 1117

findings.title: Email verification and invite codes use Math.random() with no server rate limiting
findings.description: Verification codes: Math.floor(100000 + Math.random() * 900000) — 6-digit, 900k space, non-cryptographic PRNG. Invite tokens: Math.random().toString(36) + Date.now().toString(36) (line 10060) — non-cryptographic. /api/verify-email (line 9160) has no rate limit — any number of guesses per second.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Attacker calls /api/register with target email — receives '6-digit code sent' response, triggering code generation in server memory. 2) Attacker scripts 900k calls to /api/verify-email iterating 000000..999999 — no rate limit. At 100 req/s that's ~2.5 hours worst case. At 1000 req/s (parallel) ~15 minutes. 3) First correct code activates the account. Or, if codes are shorter-lived, attacker repeats. 4) Math.random() predictability (V8 xorshift128+ state) can be recovered after a handful of observed outputs — attacker who triggers code generation for their own accounts learns the PRNG state, predicts codes for future target-account registrations.
findings.impact: Account takeover (register-then-intercept flow), invite hijack if Math.random tokens are guessable within expiration window.
findings.recommendation: 1) Replace Math.random() with crypto.randomInt(100000, 1000000) for codes and crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex') for invite tokens (already done correctly at line 3360 for password reset — copy that pattern). 2) Add server-side rate limiting on /api/verify-email, /api/login, /api/invite/accept: track attempts by (IP, email) with exponential backoff and lockout after N failures. 3) Invalidate verification codes after N failed attempts — do not allow unlimited guesses against the same code.
findings.playbook: Patch → Roll a new rate-limit library (express-rate-limit with a Redis/memory store) → Deploy
findings.verification: independently verified — Math.random() present at lines 9050/9094 for codes and 10060 for tokens; no rate limit on verify-email

findings.title: Password hashes, verification codes, and full request bodies logged in plaintext
findings.description: Line 1614 (and elsewhere): console.log of the user row from Login query includes PASSWORD_HASH. Line 9135: console.log includes the plaintext 6-digit verification code. Line 9164: console.log(JSON.stringify(req.body)) — logs entire body including verification code and user email. Line 1102: logs first 20 chars of JWT tokens (less severe but still unnecessary).
findings.exploit_scenario: Azure App Service streams stdout/stderr to Log Analytics. Anyone with Log Analytics read access (contractors, ops) sees: (a) bcrypt hashes → offline crack against weak passwords, (b) live verification codes → pre-emptive use before the legitimate user redeems, (c) email PII. If logs are ever exfiltrated or a log collection service is breached, these secrets go with them.
findings.impact: Credential-hash harvesting, code interception, PII disclosure via log pipelines.
findings.recommendation: Audit every console.log path in the login/register/verify/reset endpoints. Remove or mask: PASSWORD_HASH, verificationCode, reset codes, tokens, full req.body. Use a logger with redaction (pino with redact, winston with format) rather than raw console.log. Never log req.body unconditionally.
findings.playbook: Patch logs → Rotate existing app-log retention → Consider re-hashing weak user passwords (via forced reset) if hash exposure was possible
findings.verification: independently verified — three distinct log sites identified

findings.title: xlsx@0.18.5 prototype pollution and ReDoS — no upstream fix available
findings.description: xlsx 0.18.5 has two high-severity advisories (GHSA-4r6h-8v6p-xvw6 prototype pollution, GHSA-5pgg-2g8v-p4x9 ReDoS). No patch available from maintainer. Used in src/pages/AdminUserManagement.tsx — parses user-uploaded spreadsheets in the browser.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Attacker uploads a crafted .xlsx through the admin user-management import flow. 2) XLSX.read() pollutes Object.prototype — any subsequent admin action may behave unexpectedly (XSS, privilege escalation via polluted isAdmin check, etc.). 3) ReDoS variant stalls the admin's browser.
findings.impact: Admin-session compromise via malicious file upload. Limited to users with access to the import feature.
findings.recommendation: Migrate to a maintained alternative: exceljs (actively patched) or sheetjs Pro (paid, actively maintained). If you must keep xlsx: (a) parse in a Web Worker (isolates the prototype pollution), (b) validate file size/structure before parse, (c) limit the feature to super-admins.
findings.playbook: Plan migration → Replace import in AdminUserManagement.tsx → Test import/export flows → Deploy

findings.title: 18 dependency vulnerabilities (6 high): axios SSRF, express chain, glob command injection, dompurify bypass, @azure/identity/msal chain
findings.description: npm audit reports 6 high + 11 moderate + 1 low. Notable: axios <1.15.0 has SSRF + cloud-metadata-exfil, express high via body-parser and path-to-regexp, glob command injection, dompurify XSS bypass <3.4.0, flatted prototype pollution, @azure/msal-node → @azure/identity → tedious → mssql chain on old uuid.
findings.exploit_scenario: axios SSRF is exploitable if any server endpoint proxies user-controlled URLs (see also Finding 13 if SSRF surfaces exist). dompurify bypass matters if frontend sanitizes user HTML before render. path-to-regexp DoS possible via crafted route params.
findings.impact: Varies by dep — from DoS to SSRF to prototype pollution escalating to XSS.
findings.recommendation: Run `npm audit fix` and review breaking changes for major version bumps. Pin axios to ^1.15.0, upgrade express to latest v5 (carefully — breaking changes), replace glob with globby if only CLI feature is needed.

findings.title: Production and staging .env files target the DEV database (data contamination risk)
findings.description: All three env files (.env.development, .env.staging, .env.production) have DATABASE_URL pointing at guardian-dev-db.database.windows.net / GUARDIAN-DEV. The same password hash across all three (097f808d98609926) confirms identical credential. Azure App Service may override with real prod DB via app settings — but the committed files baked into deployment zip mean the fallback is dev.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Azure App Service env var wipe (slot swap, misconfigured deployment slot, or unset during incident) → app loads DATABASE_URL from deployment/.env which is the committed dev value. 2) Production user writes land in GUARDIAN-DEV, corrupting dev data and mixing prod PII with dev test data. 3) Alternatively this is the actual running config, meaning all tenants share one DB with no true prod separation.
findings.impact: Data loss, cross-environment PII mixing, compliance risk if PHI/PII is mis-isolated.
findings.recommendation: 1) Verify what Azure App Service actually uses by querying /api/debug/status (if safe) or checking Azure config. 2) Create a real prod DB if it doesn't exist. 3) Stop copying .env.production into the deployment zip — store all prod secrets in Azure App Service configuration blade. 4) Have the pipeline skip the '.env' copy step entirely; rely on Azure-injected env vars.
findings.playbook: Verify prod DB target → Create real prod DB if missing → Migrate data with consent → Update Azure config → Remove env-copy pipeline step
findings.verification: file inspection confirmed; same password hash across all three env files

findings.title: azure-pipelines.yml and azure-pipelines-staging.yml both deploy to GUARDIAN-STAGING
findings.description: Both pipelines trigger on `main` and both target appName: GUARDIAN-STAGING in azureSubscription AzureSP-Dev. There is no prod pipeline. Either (a) no pipeline has been configured for the real prod app (guardian-mvp-dtgph0bcd4ctdbhb), or (b) these pipelines race each other on every push, or (c) one is intentionally unused dead code. Also: both pipelines copy .env.production into the deployment .env — so 'staging' gets prod creds baked in.
findings.exploit_scenario: Race condition: push to main triggers two pipelines, both deploy simultaneously, one's zip overwrites the other mid-flight — deployment corruption. More broadly: unclear environment boundaries make rollback, rollforward, and access control unpredictable. Also: an attacker who compromises the Azure DevOps pipeline (e.g., via a compromised service connection token) gets concurrent write access to what should be two separate environments.
findings.impact: Deployment race, no prod separation, unclear audit trail.
findings.recommendation: Decide which pipeline is authoritative and delete the other. If you need both staging and prod pipelines, make them trigger on different branches (staging → main, prod → release tag) and target different apps (appName: GUARDIAN-STAGING vs GUARDIAN-PROD). Each environment should pull secrets from its own Azure App Service config, not a committed .env.
findings.playbook: Delete duplicate → Rename → Configure real prod → Test
findings.verification: both YAML files inspected at line 170-172

findings.title: 898 files including deployment/node_modules/ are tracked in git
findings.description: git ls-files deployment/ returns 898 files including node_modules/.bin/mime, node_modules/@prisma/client/runtime/binary.js, etc. Plus compiled assets (deployment/assets/index-*.js) and deployment/server.js. This was committed in c6db2fc (features/formbuilder branch) and persists.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Committed vendor code bloats history, slows clones, and allows old vulnerable versions to be retrieved even after package.json bumps. 2) A maintainer running `git checkout deployment/` restores old vendor files that may contain known-bad code. 3) Automated scanners (Dependabot) don't inspect deployment/node_modules, so stale vulns go unreported.
findings.impact: Supply chain risk persistence, repo bloat, audit blind spot.
findings.recommendation: Add deployment/ to .gitignore and `git rm -r --cached deployment/`. Regenerate the deployment folder only during pipeline builds. If the folder is genuinely needed in-repo for some legacy deploy flow, at minimum gitignore its node_modules/ subfolder.
findings.playbook: git rm -r --cached deployment/node_modules → commit → rebuild deploy pipeline → verify prod still works

findings.title: Wildcard CORS (app.use(cors())) and no Helmet/CSP/HSTS/X-Frame-Options
findings.description: cors() with default config emits Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * for all routes. No Helmet or manually-set security headers anywhere in server.cjs. No CSP, no HSTS, no X-Frame-Options.
findings.exploit_scenario: Bearer-token auth is not automatically sent cross-origin by browsers (mitigation), but any malicious site can query the API if it has a stolen token. Missing X-Frame-Options allows iframe embedding → clickjacking on admin UIs. Missing CSP amplifies any XSS in the React app into full data exfiltration.
findings.impact: Clickjacking, XSS amplification, MITM risk on HTTP.
findings.recommendation: Install helmet: `app.use(helmet())` after the cors() line. Restrict CORS to known origins: `app.use(cors({ origin: ['https://guardian-staging.azurewebsites.net', 'https://your-prod-domain.com'] }))`. Add explicit HSTS: `app.use(helmet.hsts({ maxAge: 31536000, includeSubDomains: true, preload: true }))`.
findings.playbook: Install helmet → Configure → Deploy → Verify with securityheaders.com
findings.verification: grep confirmed cors() default and no helmet/CSP mentions

findings.title: Multer file upload trusts client-supplied mimetype
findings.description: fileFilter checks file.mimetype (set by the uploading client) against an allowlist. Attackers set arbitrary Content-Type headers; they can upload a .exe or .html and claim application/pdf. No magic-byte/content sniffing. Files stored in memory (memoryStorage) — not persisted to disk, so path-traversal via filename is not directly applicable here, but the downstream code that writes attachments to the DB or a blob store may re-expose the file with the trusted mimetype.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1) Attacker uploads malicious.html renamed as report.pdf with Content-Type: application/pdf. 2) Server stores it; later, frontend or an export pipeline serves it back with the trusted mimetype. 3) If any route serves the raw content with Content-Type: text/html, stored XSS.
findings.impact: Stored XSS via uploaded attachments, depending on download/serve logic.
findings.recommendation: Use the `file-type` npm package to sniff magic bytes: `const ft = await fileTypeFromBuffer(file.buffer); if (!ALLOWED_MIME.has(ft?.mime)) throw`. Also: enforce a filename allowlist (UUIDs on the server) so user-controlled names never reach a filesystem path.
findings.playbook: Add file-type sniff → Sanitize filenames to server-generated UUIDs → Test all upload/download paths

findings.title: Multiple near-duplicate server files (server.cjs, server.js, server-production.js, server-production-backup.js) increase config drift risk
findings.file: server.cjs, server.js, server-production.js, server-production-backup.js, server-old.js, azure-server.cjs, azure-server.js
findings.description: 7+ server files at the repo root, three of which are ~670KB and nearly identical (server.cjs / server.js / server-production.js). CLAUDE.md explicitly calls out the need to manually sync endpoints across the three — this invites drift. The 407KB server-production-backup.js is dead weight.
findings.exploit_scenario: A security fix lands in server.cjs but is forgotten in server-production.js → prod continues to expose the vulnerability. Or, an old backup file is accidentally deployed via a misconfigured pipeline step.
findings.impact: Drift between dev and prod allows vulnerabilities patched in one file to persist in another.
findings.recommendation: Consolidate: have a single server file. Factor out environment differences via env vars or a small config module. Delete backups, old files, and azure-* variants. The pipeline's `cp server-production.js deployment/server.js` step is a symptom — fix by having one canonical server file.
findings.playbook: Diff the three server files → Merge into one → Delete legacy variants → Update pipeline

supply_chain_summary.install_scripts_in_prod_deps: not enumerated — has node-gyp chain likely
